title: Rubella German Measles
section: Infections Due to DNA and RNA Respiratory Viruses
authors: Alan C. Ou, Ludmila M. Perelygina, Laura A. Zimmerman, Susan E. Reef

items.title: Understanding Rubella Virus Characteristics and Transmission
items.content: The rubella virus, a single-strand RNA enveloped virus from the Matonaviridae family, measures 40–80 nm in diameter. It has one antigenic type with humans as its only known reservoir. The virus spreads via respiratory droplets, leading to primary replication in the nasopharynx and subsequent viremia.
keyPoints: Rubella virus is a member of the Matonaviridae family, genus Rubivirus.
The virus measures 40–80 nm in diameter with a ~10-kb positive-sense RNA genome.
Humans are the only known reservoir for rubella virus.
Transmission occurs through respiratory droplets.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Pathogenesis of Rubella and Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS)
items.content: Rubella virus replication begins in the nasopharynx, spreading to lymph nodes and causing viremia. In pregnant women, this can lead to placental infection and fetal organ involvement. CRS is characterized by chronic infection persisting throughout fetal development and up to 1 year post-birth.
keyPoints: Rubella virus spreads from the nasopharynx to lymph nodes and causes viremia.
In pregnant women, rubella can infect the placenta and fetal organs.
CRS involves chronic infection persisting in utero and up to 1 year after birth.
Infants with CRS can shed large quantities of virus for up to 1 year.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.title: Rubella Elimination and Current Challenges in the U.S.
items.content: The introduction of routine rubella vaccination in 1969 led to a >99% reduction in annual cases. By 2001, endemic transmission was interrupted, achieving elimination goals set for 2000. Despite this success, rubella virus importations persist, and outbreaks occur among susceptible populations, particularly adults aged 20–49 years.
keyPoints: Rubella vaccination program introduced in the U.S. in 1969
>99% reduction in annual cases since introduction of routine vaccination
Endemic transmission interrupted by 2001
66 rubella cases reported from 2010-2022, with 71% in persons aged 20–49 years
13 CRS cases reported during the same period, all from foreign-born mothers

items.title: Global Rubella and Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS) Trends
items.content: The Global Measles and Rubella Strategic Framework aims for a world free of rubella by 2030. While the Americas have eliminated endemic rubella, it remains a global issue with ~17,407 cases reported in 2022. Underreporting is common due to mild symptoms and lack of specific surveillance systems.
keyPoints: Global Measles and Rubella Strategic Framework 2021–2030
Five of six WHO regions had rubella elimination goals by 2023
~700,000 global rubella cases in 2000 reduced to 17,407 in 2022
25% of the world’s children unvaccinated against rubella in 2022
Estimated 105,000 CRS cases occurred annually worldwide in 2010

items.title: Rubella Rash Presentation and Prodrome in Different Age Groups
items.content: Acquired rubella typically presents as a generalized maculopapular rash lasting up to 3 days, often mild and sometimes subclinical. In younger children, the rash is usually the first sign of illness, whereas older children and adults may experience a prodrome including low-grade fever, malaise, and upper respiratory symptoms for 1-5 days before the rash appears.
keyPoints: Rash lasts up to 3 days
50% of cases may be subclinical or without rash
Prodrome in older children/adults: 1-5 days with low-grade fever, malaise, upper respiratory symptoms
Incubation period is 14 days (range, 12–23 days)

items.title: Congenital Rubella Syndrome and Its Implications During Pregnancy
items.content: Rubella infection during pregnancy, especially in the first trimester, can lead to serious consequences including miscarriage, fetal death, premature delivery, or congenital defects. Infants may present with physical defects related to eyes, ears, heart, and transient conditions like thrombocytopenia with purpura/petechiae.
keyPoints: Most severe when infection occurs during first trimester
Complications: miscarriage, fetal death, premature delivery, congenital defects
Common physical defects in CRS: eyes, ears, heart
Transient manifestations include thrombocytopenia with purpura/petechiae

items.title: Laboratory Confirmation of Acquired Rubella
items.content: Diagnosing acquired rubella is challenging due to its similarity with other illnesses. Laboratory confirmation through serologic methods, particularly IgM and IgG antibody testing, is essential for accurate diagnosis.
keyPoints: Serologic diagnosis relies on detecting IgM antibodies in acute-phase serum or a fourfold rise in IgG between acute- and convalescent-phase specimens.
Acute-phase specimen should be collected within 7–10 days after onset; convalescent-phase specimen ~14–21 days later.
IgM antibody may be detectable for up to 6 weeks post-infection.
Negative IgM results before day 5 of rash onset warrant retesting.
IgG avidity testing distinguishes primary infection from reinfection, with low-avidity indicating recent infection and high-avidity suggesting an older infection.

items.title: Diagnosis of Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS)
items.content: CRS diagnosis involves identifying specific clinical manifestations and using laboratory tests to confirm rubella virus presence, as most symptoms are nonspecific.
keyPoints: The classic CRS triad—cataracts, hearing impairment, heart defects—is seen in ~10% of infants with CRS.
Hearing impairment is the most common single defect in CRS.
Laboratory diagnosis includes detecting rubella virus RNA via RT-PCR or virus isolation from nasopharyngeal swabs, throat swabs, or urine.
Serologic assays for CRS include testing for serum IgM (detectable up to 6 months post-birth) and persistent IgG titers beyond expected decline rates.

items.title: Rubella Screening in Pregnant Women
items.content: Routine prenatal screening for rubella immunity is crucial to prevent congenital infections, with postpartum vaccination recommended for susceptible women.
keyPoints: Screening for rubella IgG antibodies is part of routine prenatal care in the U.S.
A positive IgG test indicates immunity against rubella.
Susceptible pregnant women should receive rubella vaccination after delivery.

items.title: Rubella Diagnosis in Pregnancy: Importance of IgM and IgG Testing
items.content: A susceptible pregnant woman exposed to rubella should be tested for IgM antibodies. If positive, low-avidity IgG testing confirms infection during pregnancy. This helps assess fetal risk, especially since the first 10 weeks pose a 90% CRS risk.
keyPoints: IgM antibody test followed by low-avidity IgG to confirm rubella infection during pregnancy
Risk of Congenital Rubella Syndrome (CRS) is 90% if infection occurs within the first 10 weeks of gestation
Rubella IgM testing not recommended for asymptomatic pregnant women without exposure history
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Therapeutic Approach to Rubella in Pregnancy
items.content: No specific therapy exists for rubella. Symptomatic treatment is appropriate, but immunoglobulin does not prevent infection or guarantee safety against CRS and should be used cautiously.
keyPoints: Symptom-based treatment (e.g., fever, arthralgia) is recommended
Immunoglobulin does not prevent rubella post-exposure; it may modify symptoms
Consider immunoglobulin if pregnancy termination is not an option, administered within 72 hours of exposure
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.title: Rubella Prevention through Vaccination
items.content: Vaccination with a rubella-containing vaccine is the most effective prevention method. One dose induces seroconversion in ≥95% of individuals aged ≥1 year, providing long-term immunity.
keyPoints: Rubella vaccination prevents CRS effectively; one dose achieves ≥95% seroconversion
Routine immunization schedule: first MMR dose at 12–15 months and second dose at 4–6 years
Avoid RCV during pregnancy due to theoretical risk, though no CRS cases reported in follow-up studies
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.question: Which family does the Rubella virus belong to?
items.explanation: Rubella virus is a member of the Matonaviridae family.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.question: What are the two glycoproteins found in the spike-like projections of the Rubella virus?
items.explanation: The spike-like projections contain two glycoproteins, E1 and E2.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.question: How long can infants with congenital rubella syndrome shed the virus after birth?
items.explanation: Infants with CRS may shed large quantities of virus up to 1 year of age.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.question: When did the largest recent rubella epidemic occur in the United States?
items.explanation: The largest recent rubella epidemic in the U.S. occurred during 1964–1965.

items.question: What percentage of rubella cases reported from 2010 to 2022 were among persons aged 20–49 years?
items.explanation: During 2010–2022, 71% of rubella cases reported were in persons aged 20–49 years.

items.question: What was the estimated number of congenital rubella syndrome (CRS) cases globally per year as of 2010?
items.explanation: In 2010, it was estimated that 105,000 CRS cases occurred annually worldwide.

items.question: What is the usual duration of a generalized maculopapular rash in acquired rubella?
items.explanation: The rash usually lasts up to 3 days.

items.question: Which lymph nodes are commonly affected during the second week after exposure to rubella?
items.explanation: Lymphadenopathy, particularly occipital and postauricular, may be noted during the second week.

items.question: What is a common complication of acquired rubella in infected adults?
items.explanation: Arthralgia and arthritis are common complications, especially in women.

items.question: Which laboratory method is most accurate for the serologic diagnosis of acute rubella infection?
options: Enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay IgM capture technique
items.explanation: The enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay IgM capture technique is considered most accurate for serologic diagnosis of rubella.

items.question: What indicates a recent infection with rubella virus in the context of avidity testing?
options: Persistence of rubella serum IgG titer beyond expected decline
items.explanation: Low-avidity antibodies indicate a recent infection with the rubella virus.

items.question: When should specimens for RT-PCR be collected to diagnose rubella?
options: Within 7–10 days after onset of illness
1–3 days after symptom onset, but no later than 7 days
Any time during the prodromal period
items.explanation: Specimens for RT-PCR should be collected as soon after symptom onset as possible, preferably within 1–3 days but no later than 7 days.

items.question: In the diagnosis of congenital rubella syndrome (CRS), what is a serologic criterion used to confirm CRS?
options: Presence of IgM antibodies for up to 6 months
Rubella serum IgG titer persisting beyond the expected decline
Rise in IgG antibody titer between acute and convalescent phases
items.explanation: A rubella serum IgG titer that does not decline at the expected rate is used to confirm CRS.

items.question: Which test is recommended to confirm acute rubella infection in a pregnant woman who tests positive for IgM antibodies?
items.explanation: Low-avidity IgG antibodies are tested to confirm acute rubella infection in pregnant women who test positive for IgM.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the risk of delivering an infant with Congenital Rubella Syndrome if a woman is infected during the first 10 weeks of gestation?
items.explanation: The risk of delivering an infant with CRS is 90% when infection occurs in the first 10 weeks of gestation.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: Why is rubella IgM antibody testing not recommended for pregnant women with no history of illness or contact?
options: It has high sensitivity but low specificity.
It can lead to false-positive results.
items.explanation: Rubella IgM antibody testing is not recommended due to the potential for false-positive results in asymptomatic women.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.question: What is the most effective method of preventing acquired rubella and Congenital Rubella Syndrome?
items.explanation: The most effective method is vaccination with an RCV.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.subtopic: Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.statement: There are multiple antigenic types of rubella virus.
items.explanation: There is only one antigenic type of rubella virus.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.subtopic: Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY
items.statement: The pathology of congenital rubella syndrome in the infected fetus involves almost all organs.
items.explanation: Almost all organs are found to be infected in CRS.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · Rubella (German Measles) | ETIOLOGY | PATHOGENESIS AND PATHOLOGY

items.statement: Rubella is no longer considered an endemic disease in the United States as of 2004.
items.explanation: In 2004, a panel agreed that rubella was no longer endemic in the U.S.

items.statement: The number of rubella cases reported worldwide increased from 700,000 in 2000 to over 1 million in 2022.
items.explanation: The number of rubella cases declined from ~700,000 in 2000 to 17,407 in 2022.

items.statement: The incubation period for rubella is typically between 12 to 23 days.
items.explanation: The incubation period is 14 days (range, 12–23 days).

items.statement: Congenital rubella syndrome primarily affects the liver and kidneys.
items.explanation: CRS most commonly relates to defects in the eyes, ears, and heart.

items.statement: Rubella virus can be detected in urine specimens for up to 2 weeks after rash onset.
items.explanation: Rubella virus is typically detected in the nasopharynx during the prodromal period and for as long as 2 weeks after rash onset, but not specifically in urine.

items.statement: IgM antibodies may be detectable for up to 6 weeks after rubella virus infection.
items.explanation: After rubella virus infection, IgM antibody can indeed be detectable for up to 6 weeks.

items.statement: Immunoglobulin administration can prevent rubella virus infection after exposure.
items.explanation: Immunoglobulin does not prevent rubella virus infection and is not recommended as routine postexposure prophylaxis.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.statement: One dose of a rubella-containing vaccine induces seroconversion in ≥95% of persons aged ≥1 year.
items.explanation: One dose of an RCV induces seroconversion in ≥95% of individuals aged 1 year and older.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: Laboratory documentation is the only reliable way to confirm acute rubella disease.
items.reason: Rubella can mimic many illnesses with rashes, presenting varied clinical manifestations and high rates of subclinical and mild disease.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains why laboratory documentation is necessary for confirmation.

items.assertion: Rubella virus isolation can be used to diagnose rubella.
items.reason: Viral RNA detection in a reverse-transcriptase polymerase chain reaction (RT-PCR) assay is the usual method for diagnosing rubella.
items.explanation: Both statements are true, but RT-PCR is usually preferred over virus isolation for diagnosis.

items.assertion: Women known to be pregnant should not receive rubella-containing vaccines.
items.reason: There is a theoretical risk of transmission of the live attenuated vaccine virus to the developing fetus.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION

items.assertion: The lifetime medical costs for children with CRS range from $11,255 in low-income countries to $934,000 in high-income countries.
items.reason: This cost variation is due to differences in healthcare systems and resources available.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason does not explain why there's a cost difference.
items.reference: Rubella German Measles · DIAGNOSIS (part 2) | Rubella | ☐ PREVENTION
